title: Fuller - Forms and Limits of Adjudication
description: Lon L. Fuller — The Forms and Limits of Adjudication (92 Harv. L. Rev. 353 (1978))
show_notes:
Lon L. Fuller — The Forms and Limits of Adjudication (92 Harv. L. Rev. 353 (1978))

Lon Fuller's classic essay analyzes adjudication as a distinct form of social ordering, defined by its characteristic mode of participation: the affected party participates by presenting proofs and reasoned arguments, in contrast to the negotiation of contract or the voting of elections. Fuller argues that this structure gives adjudication both its moral force and its intrinsic limits — it is well suited to yes-or-no questions of right and duty but ill suited to 'polycentric' problems, in which many interacting variables mean that resolving one issue unpredictably reshuffles all the others. Such tasks, he contends, are better handled by managerial direction or contract than by courts.
